metadata.title: Why Your Examples Teach More Than Your Instructions
metadata.purpose: Explain that unannotated examples teach every feature simultaneously — structure, register, sentence length, hedging — so Claude copies features the user never intended to share.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: INK = instructions / intended features; CRIMSON = unintended features that get copied
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO few-shot learning benchmark research, NO transformer attention mechanism, NO extended in-context learning theory. Mechanism only: unannotated examples teach all observable features equally; annotation isolates which features to copy.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: She pasted her memo as a format example. Claude returned something perfectly matching the format — and the casual tone she never meant to include.

card.copy: Why your examples teach more than your instructions
card.sub: the features you never meant to copy

beats.narration_text: Priya runs communications for a research institute. She needs a formal board report. She pastes a casual internal memo as a format reference and types: write the board report in the same style.
beats.scene_description: A professional woman at her desk, looking at two documents side by side on a laptop screen, newsprint editorial treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, professional woman at a modern office desk, two documents visible on laptop screen, focused expression, editorial newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print, collage style

beats.narration_text: She wanted Claude to copy the paragraph structure. Instead it copied the casual register, hedging language, and short sentence rhythm too. Why did the example teach more than she intended?

card.copy: Why did the example teach more than she intended?

beats.narration_text: Instructions in prompts are explicit — they describe what you want. But an example is an instruction in disguise. It teaches by demonstration across every feature it contains, whether or not you labeled any of them.

A memo has observable features. Structure: topics in paragraphs. Register: casual, hedged. Sentence length: short. Tone: conversational. When you paste it without annotation, all of these carry equal weight as teaching signal.

Claude pattern-matches across all of them at once. It cannot know which features you consider essential and which are incidental unless you say. So it treats the example as a complete specification — and learns everything in it.
Structure passes through. But so does register, sentence length, hedging density, and vocabulary. The board report returns sounding like the memo — because it was taught by the memo.
Annotation changes this. You tell Claude which features to copy — and which to ignore. The example stays; the specification narrows it.
Priya adds three lines to her prompt: copy the paragraph-per-topic structure. Not the casual tone. Not the hedging language. Now only one feature carries signal. The rest are excluded.
This holds for any example — a quote you liked, a competitor's press release, a paper abstract. Each contains dozens of features. Without annotation, all of them teach.

Marco writes a product blog post. He pastes a competitor's post he admires and asks Claude to match the style. The output is punchy, short-sentenced, and uses three exclamation points — which were in the competitor's post, which he hates. He never labeled them as features to exclude.

He adds one line: copy the punchy short sentences. Not the exclamation points — we never use those. Next output: punchy, short, zero exclamation points. The example stayed. The annotation narrowed it.

The practice: before you paste any example, write two sentences. Which features should Claude copy? Which should it ignore? Those two sentences are your annotation — and they are the actual specification.
An unannotated example teaches every feature it contains. Label which to copy, which to skip — and your example becomes a precise instruction instead of a full transfer.
